Java has a vast set of interfaces and classes for creating, reading, and modifying files. If you have never worked with files before, it might look intimidating at first but don't worry, I will start with a few simple...

Exam Objectives Covered

  • Read and write console and file data using I/O Streams
  • Serialize and de-serialize Java objects
  • Create, traverse, read, and write Path objects and their properties using java.nio.file API
ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
392

Chapter 19 Using Java I/O API

Exam Objectives

  • Read and write console and file data using I/O Streams
  • Serialize and de-serialize Java objects
  • Create, traverse, read, and write Path objects and their properties using java.nio.file API

19.1 I/O Basics☝

Java has a vast set of interfaces and classes for creating, reading, and modifying files. If you have never worked with files before, it might look intimidating at first but don’t worry, I will start with a few simple examples to give you a feel of the API and then get into the details required for the exam.

First, let’s get our terminology straight.

19.1.1 I/O streams☝

In the Java world, input and output of data to and from a Java program is done in the terms of bytes flowing into the program or out of the program. This flow of bytes is called “stream”. So, a stream of bytes coming into a program is an “input” stream and a stream of bytes going out of a program is an “output” stream. A program reads the bytes coming in using an input stream and it writes the bytes that are meant to go out of the program using an output stream.

Note that usage of the word stream here has nothing to do with the Java Stream API. It is used only to evoke a mental picture of a stream of water because a stream of bytes

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
393

behaves quite similarly.

Since it is sometimes too tedious for a programmer to deal in raw bytes, the Java I/O library provides classes that wrap raw byte streams into streams of more meaningful data such primitive types, text, and objects. Thus, for example, a programmer could use a wrapper stream to read characters instead of reading raw bytes. The wrapper stream implements the logic of converting raw bytes into character data and returns a stream of characters instead of bytes. One could again wrap the character stream into a stream that returns lines of text. The same applies for writing bytes. A programmer could directly write lines of text using an output stream that automatically converts lines of text into raw bytes, which can then be written to the destination.

Just like a stream of water, a stream of bytes has to start from somewhere (source) and end at somewhere (destination). The source or the destination of a data stream could be a file, a network socket, or even an in-memory array. There are classes in the Java standard library that let you work with commonly used sources and destinations. Thus, once you identity the source (or destination) and the format of the data, all you have to do is to chain and wrap existing classes to achieve your goal. This is illustrated by the following figure.

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
394

In the above image, observe that a Java program gets raw bytes from the source through a raw input stream and translates the raw bytes into data types needed by the program. Similarly, the program writes data types using a higher level stream, which converts them into bytes. The higher level stream then uses a raw output stream to write the bytes to the destination. The source or the destination do not care about how the bytes are interpreted or generated by the Java program.

Blocking vs Non-blocking I/O☝

For a long time, Java had only the classes in java.io package to perform I/O related tasks. However, these classes implement what is known as “blocking” I/O operations. They are called blocking because once you call a method to read or write data using these classes, that call will block until the operation is complete or until the stream has ended. For example, if you try to read data from a remote file or from a network socket, the thread on which the read method is being executed may be blocked for a long time and will not be available to perform other tasks. Although this approach is fine for many applications, it doesn’t scale well. Imagine a server that is handling millions of connections from users and reads the data sent by them using

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
395

blocking I/O. If a thread is blocked on a read operation for even a few milliseconds, the server will grind to a halt very soon because there are only a limited number of threads. It is for this reason that older web servers used “thread pooling” to process requests.

To overcome this limitation, Java introduced a non-blocking I/O (aka NIO) library in Java 1.4 and updated it in Java 7 (aka NIO2). These classes are packaged in the java.nio package. Working with non-blocking I/O is substantially more complicated than working with blocking I/O but fortunately, this topic is not included in the exam. However, the nio library also includes the java.nio.file package which includes a few classes such as Files and Paths that are very helpful even while dealing with the blocking I/O and the functionality provided by this package is on the exam.

19.1.2 Reading and writing binary data☝

The following program illustrates how you can read/write raw bytes directly from/to a file.

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
396

The above program is a good starting point because even though it is fully functional, it has several shortcomings that necessitate the need for many of the important classes and interfaces of Java I/O API. Observe the following points about the above program:

  1. The concept of raw input stream and raw output stream is represented by abstract classes InputStream and OutputStream respectively.
  2. Since the source and destination of our input and output streams are files, we are using FileInputStream and FileOutputStream classes provided by the Java I/O library to read from and write to the files. They extend InputStream and OutputStream respectively.
  3. The source of our input is a file located at c:\temp\test1.png and the destination of our output is another file located at c:\temp\test2.png . Note that these paths are Windows specific. If you are on Linux, you will have to use something like /home/myname/temp/test1.png . If you are thinking that the paths are totally making the program platform dependent, then you are right. They indeed are. There is a java.io.File class that helps us avoid platform specific path separators in the code. Furthermore, there is a Path API in java.nio.file package that helps us manipulate paths and interact with the file system in a platform independent manner.
  4. Although the read() method reads a byte from the input stream, the return type of this method is int , which is why the variable bite has been declared to be of type int . Only the lower order 8 bits of the returned integer contain the actual byte read from the input stream. To get that value, we need to cast the
ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
397
  1. returned value to byte. Recall that casting an int value to byte removes 24 most significant bits (i.e. three higher order bytes) and leaves the 8 least significant bits of the given value intact.
  2. We keep reading from the input stream until we get a -1. A -1 indicates that the stream has ended and there is no more data available to read from the input stream.
  3. Encountering exceptional situations while performing I/O is common. For example, the file that you are trying to open may not be present or the file that you are trying to write to may be marked read only by the OS. There are several specialized exception classes such as FileNotFoundException or EOFException defined in the standard library for this purpose, but all of them are rooted under the checked exception class java.io.IOException and so, the throws IOException clause in main satisfies all of them.
  4. The writing part is unremarkable. It is just writing a thousand bytes one by one.
  5. Finally, we use the close() method to close the input and output streams. Closing I/O streams using the close() method is important because it allows any system resources associated with the streams such as file locks to be reclaimed by the OS. Closing an output stream also flushes all data remaining in the output pipeline to the destination. Since InputStream and OutputStream implement AutoCloseable, the preferable approach is to let the try-with-resources statement close the streams automatically.

The Java API designers decided to return an int from read() instead of byte because an extra value was needed to signify the end of the stream. The total number of values returned by the method is 257, which includes 256 values (00000000 to 11111111) represented by a byte and an extra value of -1 to denote the end of the stream. Since a byte cannot represent 257 values, a larger data type was needed. This could have been achieved using a short as well, but int was used to remain consistent with the C language.

Similarly, even though the write(int) method accepts an int parameter, only the lower 8 bits are actually written to the file. Although byte would have sufficed in this case, int was used

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
398

to be consistent with C.

Generally, reading data byte by byte is not too useful. For example, if you want to manipulate image data, you would need to load the complete image file in program memory and then process it. Similarly, writing to a file one byte at a time is quite inefficient. InputStream and OutputStream have a few methods that help you perform I/O operations in bulk. Here is an example that reads data from an InputStream and writes it to an OutputStream in chunks of 1024 bytes:

In the above code, we pass a byte array of size 1024 to the read method. The read method populates the byte array with the bytes read from the input stream and returns the number of bytes that it actually read. It overwrites the byte array with the newly read bytes starting from index 0 .

To write the bytes to the output stream, we are using OutputStream 's write(byte[] b, int offset, int len) method, which writes len  number of bytes from the given byte array starting from the index given by the offset parameter (which is 0 in our example). Note that the number of bytes read may be less than 1024 in the last iteration of the while loop and in that case, the byte array will still have old values read in the previous iteration at the index position indicated by bytesRead and beyond. Since we are only writing bytesRead number of values starting from index 0 , those old values will not be written to the output stream.

The size of 1024 is completely arbitrary. You can use any size that the program

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
399

environment permits. If you are reading bytes from a small file (may be about a few MBs), you may also use InputStream 's available() method to get the number of bytes that can be read immediately without blocking and then create a byte buffer of that size. Reading large chunks at once is not recommended because that may hog all of your program memory.

If this looks like too much work just to copy data from one stream to another then you are in luck! Java 9 has added a transferTo(OutputStream) method in InputStream that can achieve the above in just one line.

19.1.3 Summary of byte streams☝

The following image shows the important input streams that work with bytes.

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
400

The following image shows the important output streams that work with bytes.

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
401

The following are a few points worth noting about the classes shown above:

  1. InputStream and OutputStream are the root classes for all input and output streams respectively. They are abstract and they contain most of the methods that we normally use while dealing with low level input and output. 

  2. Specialized classes such as ObjectInputStream (and its corresponding ObjectOutputStream) and BufferedInputStream (and its corresponding BufferedOutputStream) do not interact with the data source (or the data sink) such as a file directly. They take an existing input stream (or an existing

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
402

  1. output stream) and build additional functionality on top of those streams. That is why such streams are called “higher-level” streams. 

  2. Higher level streams do not care about where the data actually comes from or goes to. It is the job of the lower level streams such as FileInputStream / FileOutputStream to deal with the actual data source or the sink. 

Although not an official naming rule, a good trick to find out whether an I/O stream class constructor takes an input or an output stream as an argument is to see whether the name of the class reveals an actual data source/sink. If the name of an I/O stream class reveals an actual data source or sink, it is a lower level stream and it does not take an input or an output stream as an argument during instantiation. For example, FileInputStream, and ByteArrayInputStream reveal their data sources (a file and a byte array respectively) and are therefore, lower level streams. On the other hand, ObjectInputStream, BufferedInputStream, and DataInputStream reveal the type of the data that they deal with (Object, byte buffer, and primitive data, respectively) instead of the source or the sink of the data and are therefore, higher level streams. They do take an InputStream (or an OutputStream) as an argument during instantiation.

19.1.4 Reading and writing character data☝

You know that the actual data stored in a file system is a series of zeros and ones. It is our program that reads those bits and interprets them as something more meaningful such as characters. Thus, you can use raw InputStream and OutputStream classes to read raw bytes and interpret those bytes into characters on your own using some character encoding such as ASCII. However, converting bytes into characters is not always as trivial as interpreting a byte as an ASCII character. There are hundreds of languages and thousands of characters that exist and they cannot all be mapped directly to values of a byte. That is why modern character encodings use multiple

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
403

bytes to store a single character. Fortunately, Java provides InputStreamReader and OutputStreamWriter concrete classes that perform the conversion as per the given character encoding. An InputStreamReader reads raw bytes from an InputStream and converts them into characters on the fly. Similarly, an OutputStreamWriter converts a stream of characters to a stream of bytes on the fly and pipes the resulting stream of bytes to an OutputStream as shown in the following code:

Observe the following points in the above code:

  1. We are using FileInputStream and FileOutputStream to get raw input and output streams from and to a file. We are then using them to create InputStreamReader and OutputStreamWriter instances so that we can read/write characters instead of bytes from/to the file.
  2. We are using UTF-8 as the character encoding while writing and reading bytes
ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
404
  1. to and from the file. You do not need to know the details of any character encoding for the OCP exam but it is good to know about some of the commonly used character encodings such as UTF-8 , UTF-16 , and ISO-8859-1 . UTF-8 is the most popular of all and is also the recommended encoding for English only as well as for multilingual text because if its efficiency and its compatibility with ASCII.
  2. Calling close() on InputStreamReader or OutputStreamWriter also closes the underlying FileInputStream and FileOutputStream instances.

Following the pattern of wrapping a lower level stream to create a higher level stream, we can read and write lines of text instead of single characters by wrapping an InputStreamReader into a BufferedReader and an OutputStreamWriter into a BufferedWriter as follows:

The above code prints:

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
405

Observe the following points in the above code and output:

  1. A total of three lines are read from the file. The third line is an empty string (it is not null). This is because a line is considered to be terminated by any one of a line feed (\n ), a carriage return (\r ), a carriage return followed immediately by a line feed (\r\n ), or by reaching the end of the file. Note that \n is not a string containing two characters. It is just one character whose numeric value is 10. Similarly, \r is just one character whose numeric value is 13. When you write these characters in a file, they show up as line breaks in a text editor. To prevent the compiler from treating them as line breaks in our source code, we use escape sequences \r and \n . In the above code, the first line is terminated by \r\n , the second line is terminated by \n , and the third line is terminated by \r . There is no fourth line in the file.
  2. We keep on reading the lines until the BufferedReader returns null .
  3. We are using the try-with resources statement with multiple resources and var  declarations. This is typical while using I/O streams.

Java also provides concrete classes named FileReader and FileWriter that extend InputStreamReader and OutputStreamWriter respectively for reading character data. Here is a sample program that shows how to use these classes:

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
406

The above program is similar to the previous ones except that it uses an instance of the java.nio.charset.Charset class instead of a String to specify the character encoding.

Charset is a utility class that contains the mapping between raw byte data ("code units", to be precise but you can ignore that for the exam) and the characters.

Character data always requires an encoding and decoding mechanism to convert characters into raw bytes and vice-versa. The rules for this interpretation are defined by a character encoding. Although it is usually possible to read and write character data without specifying a character encoding explicitly, it is a bad idea to do so because not specifying a character encoding explicitly just means that some default character encoding is applied by the underlying API implicitly. Any change in the default encoding may render the existing character data completely incomprehensible. A change in the default character encoding could happen because of migration or upgradation of the underlying data store such as the file system or the database.

Prior to Java 11, FileReader and FileWriter did not take a Charset as an argument during instantiation. They used the default encoding as determined from the environment in which the program is run. This behavior leaves the programmer unaware of the conversion mechanism and renders the data

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
407

potentially non-portable. That is why the preferred way to read and write character data prior to Java 11 was to use InputStreamReader and OutputStreamWriter classes, which take the name of the character encoding as an argument.

In Java 18, FileReader and FileWriter and several other classes were updated to use UTF- 8 as the default character encoding instead of relying on the default character encoding of the environment. Although this change does make the code more portable, it is still better to specify the character encoding explicitly.

19.1.5 Summary of character streams☝

The following image shows the important input streams that work with characters.

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
408

The following image shows the important output streams that work with characters.

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
409

The following are a few points worth noting about the classes shown above:

  1. Reader and Writer are the root classes for all character based input and output streams respectively. They are abstract and they contain most of the methods that we normally use while dealing with low level character based input

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
410

  1. and output.

  2. InputStreamReader and OutputStreamWriter classes act as a bridge between a byte stream and a character stream. They convert a byte stream to a character stream (and vice- versa) by using the specified (or the default) character encoding.

  3. Some classes such as BufferedReader and BufferedWriter require an instance of a bridge class (i.e. InputStreamReader or OutputStreamWriter) as a constructor argument, while others such as FileReader, FileWriter, and PrintWriter can be created even without such bridge classes. Such classes create an intermediate InputStreamReader or OutputStreamWriter object internally to perform the conversion between bytes and characters.

  4. The above summary does not include PrintStream. Although it is used very often to print just about everything to the console (using System.out), PrintStream is not a Writer. I will talk about it separately.

19.1.6 Advanced I/O stream operations☝

Rewinding a stream☝

Generally, data from an input stream can only be read once. You cannot "rewind" a stream and reread the same data again. However, some input streams that use internal data buffers may be able to provide this functionality. This functionality comes in handy while implementing parsers that need to rewind to a previous location if they encounter a specific control character. For example, the following codes reads a text file and identifies words enclosed within < and >. The logic is as follows. We "mark" the stream when we encounter a < and start counting the characters. We count till we encounter a >. As soon as we encounter a >, we reset the stream back to the marked location and read the number of characters indicated by the count in a character buffer.

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
411

If the test.txt file contains <html> </html> , the above code will print html /html .

Here are a few points worth nothing about the above code:

  1. The markSupported method tells you whether the underlying stream supports mark and reset.
  2. The mark method takes an int parameter named markAheadLimit . It sets the maximum number of characters you can read safely after which the stream may invalidate the mark. In the above code, we have passed 256, which means, if we read more than 256 characters after setting the mark, we may not be able to
ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
412
  1. reset the stream back to that mark. The stream will throw an IOException if we reset the stream after the stream has already invalidated the mark.
  2. The skip method skips the stream forward by the specified number of characters.

Note that the above example uses a Reader (which means, it is a character stream) but this functionality works the same way for an InputStream (which means, it is a byte stream) as well. The only difference is that in case of a byte stream, the arguments to mark and skip methods are interpreted as the number of bytes (instead of number of characters).

19.1.7 Quiz☝

Q1. Given that test1.txt contains the following two lines of text:

(Assume that the first line is terminated by \r\n . What will be the contents of test2.txt after the following code is run?

Select 1 correct option.

A.

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
413

B.

C.

D.

Correct answer is B.

There are a total of 12 characters in test1.txt (don’t forget the two characters used for terminating the first line). In the first iteration of the while loop, the read method fills buff with the first 5 chars, i.e., hello. Observe that we have not specified the offset and the length arguments to the write method. So, it writes all of the characters in buff to test2.txt.

Thus, hello is written to the file. In the second iteration, the read method reads the next 5 chars, i.e., \r\nwor, which are then written to the file. Now there are only 2 more characters left to be read from the file. Thus, in the third iteration, the read method reads only those characters and puts them in the first two locations of buff. The remaining three locations in buff are unaffected and still contain the characters read in the previous iteration, i.e., wor.

So, buff now contains ldwor. Thus, the write method writes ldwor to the file. The fourth iteration does not happen because the read method returns -1.

Had we used fw.write(buff, 0, count) instead of fw.write(buff), the program would have copied the contents correctly.

19.1.8 Understanding file names and paths☝

I am sure you have been managing files in your computer using the File Explorer

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
414

since a long time and you don’t need an elementary level tutorial on how to organize files and folders (aka directories). Even so, let me talk about a few terms that will be used extensively while learning about the Path API in the next section.

Files, Directories, and Folders☝

When you look at your file system through the file explorer, you see folders and files. Folders contain files and/or other folders, while files contain the data. Observe that a folder may contain multiple files and folders but a file or a folder can be contained exactly within one folder. This is what makes it a hierarchical file system. If you use the command line (or the shell), you can observe the same hierarchical structure in terms of files and directories using the dir command on Windows (or ls on Linux). At the top of a hierarchical file system is the root of that file system. In Windows, a root is denoted by a drive letter such as c: and in Linux, it is denoted by just a forward slash (/).

In the Java world, however, there is a single abstraction called "file" for files as well as directories. A file could be a regular file or it could be a directory (aka a folder). You can check what it is by inquiring its "attributes". So, if a file is a directory, you can invoke directory related operations on it such as retrieve the names of the files that it contains and if it is a regular file, you can read/write data from/to that file using various type of streams that you saw earlier.

File name☝

The name of a file is just a string that identifies the file within its parent directory. Conventionally, names for regular files end with a dot and a multi lettered extension such as .txt or .java while names of directories do not.

Pathname and Abstract pathname☝

An operating system uses a pathname to identify a file, while Java uses an abstract pathname for the same. The JVM converts an abstract pathname to the real pathname and vice-versa as and when required. The conversion logic depends on the operating system.

An abstract pathname is a series of zero or more file names separated by a system specific separator character. On Windows the separator character is back slash (\),

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
415

while on Linux and MacOS, it is forward slash (/). It is not necessary for an abstract pathname to refer to an actual file in the file system, but if you want to be able to convert it to a real pathname, every component except the last one of an abstract pathname must correspond to a directory. This makes sense because a file cannot contain other files, only a directory can.

Ideally, you should use the platform specific separator character while writing pathnames in your code (if you want to hardcode them in your code at all) but on Windows, the JVM automatically converts forward slashes to back slashes and so, if you use forward slashes in your code, the path will be acceptable on Windows as well as on Unix like machines. You will learn better ways to work with paths in a platform independent manner soon.

Absolute path and relative path☝

To access a file in a hierarchical file system, we need to know not just the name of the file but also the names of its containing folders, all the way up to the root. So, if a pathname includes all the names starting from the root down to the file that you want to access, it is an absolute path.

For example, c:\a\b\c\test.txt is an absolute path because we can start from the root c:\ and step inside the folder named a, then in b, and c to reach the test.txt file.

Similarly, on Linux, since the root is denoted by /, /a/b/c/test.txt would be an absolute path.

A relative path, on the other hand, is not sufficient in itself to identify a file in a file system. It can only identify a file when used in relation to another path whose location is already known.

For example, b\c\test.txt is not sufficient in itself to locate the test.txt file. However, if we are told that this path is relative to c:\a, then we can figure out its exact location. Thus, any path that does not start with a root is a relative path.

Current directory☝

The current directory is the path from where a program is run. For example, if you run your Java program from c:\temp, then c:\temp will be the current directory irrespective of where the class file being executed is located. When you execute a Jar file directly by double clicking on it, Windows sets the current directory as directory in

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
416

which the jar file is located. On older Windows and Java versions, the current directory is c:\windows\system32 .

Knowing the current directory is important because all relative paths are resolved against the current directory by default. So, if your program tries to open a file whose relative path is a\test.txt file and if the current directory is c:\temp , then the JVM will expect the file to be located at c:\temp\a\test.txt .

Dot (.) and dot dot (..)☝

You need to be aware that all directories in Unix and DOS based files systems automatically get two children named . and .. , where . refers to the same directory as the one in which it exists and .. refers to its parent directory. For example, if you execute " dir . " command (without the double quotes) in c:\temp , you will see a listing of the files present in c:\temp and if you run " dir .. " command, you will see a listing of files present in the parent of temp , i.e., c:\ . You will observe the same on a Linux machine as well. The reason and purpose of their existence has a lot to do with how a file system is managed by an OS, but you are not required to learn those details for the exam. You just need to know how they affect a pathname. A dot means, stay in the same directory, while a dot dot means, go a level up. So, for example, c:\temp\a\.. means, starting from c: , go to temp , then to a , and then back to the parent of a , which means, you are back to c:\temp . Similarly, if do cd .. while in c:\temp on the command prompt, you will be in c: .

Canonical path☝

A canonical path is an absolute path that doesn’t contain any redundant path fragments. For example, c:\temp\a\..\test.txt is an absolute path but if you follow this path, you will go from c: to temp , then from temp to a , then back to temp , and finally to test.txt .

The presence of .. cancels out the a that appears before it. Thus, a\.. forms a kind of loop in the path. A canonical path irons out all such loops and gives you a straight path from the root to the destination.

Some operating systems allow you to create symbolic links to other files in the file system. A canonical path resolves such links as well, meaning, it shows the real path instead of the symbolic link.

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
417

19.1.9 Using the java.io.File class☝

You have already seen most of the important classes of the java.io package. The only other class that you need to know about from this package is the File class. It is an immutable class whose objects represent a file or a directory path name. The purpose of this class is not to operate on the data inside the file but to work with the file itself in a platform independent manner.

Creation, renaming and deletion

The following code illustrates how to create, rename, and delete the actual file in the file system using methods of the File class:

Observe that a File object representing any file can be created irrespective of whether the file actually exists or not in the file system. The createNewFile method returns true if it was able to create an empty file in the file system. It could fail (and return false) if the file already exists or if there is a security restriction.  The renameTo , makeDir , makeDirs , and delete methods work similarly.

Notably, the File class does not have methods to copy and move a file. The renameTo method can be used to move the file but it may fail if the destination is on a different file system or if the destination already exists. The

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
418

java.nio.file.Files class provides a more comprehensive way to do these operations.

Listing a directory

You may use a File object to list the contents of a directory if the File object corresponds to a directory in the file system. For example, the following code prints names of all the files present in c:\temp directory:

Using platform independent name separators

In the previous code examples, I used Windows specific paths to files such as "c:\temp\test1.txt" and "c:\temp". These path strings won't work on a Linux machine because Linux uses forward slash ( / ) as the separator character. Furthermore, the root of a file system in Linux is just /. Linux doesn't use drive letters such as c:. The File class has two public static fields for this purpose:

  1. public static char separatorChar : It is the system-dependent default name-separator character. On Windows it is back slash ( '\\' ) and on Linux, it is forward slash ( '/' ).
  2. public static String separator : A String version of separatorChar for convenience. It contains the same character as separatorChar.

So, if I write the path string "c:\temp\test.txt" as File.separator + "temp" + File.separator + "test.txt", it will work on Windows as well as on Linux because it will be translated to \temp\test.txt if the program is run on a Windows machine and to /temp/test.txt if it is run on a Linux machine. Yes, the

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
419

translation on Windows is missing the drive letter c: , but unless you want to target a file on a different drive than the one from which your program is executed, a drive letter is not required for specifying absolute paths. Any path that starts with the separator character is assumed to be starting from the root of the file system (on all OSs). Thus, on a Windows machine, if your path starts with a \ and your current directory is on the C drive, then \temp\test.txt will refer to c:\temp\test.txt .

The File class has two more public static fields for separating paths in a platform independent manner. These are:

  1. public static char pathSeparatorChar : It is the system-dependent path-separator character. On windows, it is ; and on Linux, it is : .
  2. public static String pathSeparator : A String version of pathSeparatorChar for convenience. It contains the same character as pathSeparatorChar.

Although completely beyond the scope of the exam, it is good to know that these fields come in handy when you want to dynamically execute a command that expects a list of paths as an argument.

Getting path information

The File class has methods such as getAbsolutePath and getCanonicalPath to get the absolute path and the canonical path respectively of the file. They can be used as follows:

The above code uses a relative path while creating the File object ( . is relative because it does not start with the root). This path is relative to the current directory. Thus, effectively, the above code shows you the current directory. If you run the above code from c:\temp , it will print C:\temp\. C:\temp . Observe that the canonical path does not contain a dot at the end because the dot is redundant.

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
420

Getting file attributes☝

The File class lets you enquire about its properties of the file through methods such as isDirectory() , isFile() , isHidden() , and lastModified() .

The File class has been in existence since Java 1.0 and is usually sufficient for routine things.

However, since the exam does not focus on the File class much, I am not going into the details of all its methods. I suggest you to browse through the JavaDoc for the File class and write a few test programs to see how they work.

19.2 The java.nio.file package☝

The java.nio.file package provides a more comprehensive and platform independent access to the file system than the java.io.File class. It does so, by formally abstracting the concepts of file system, path, and file attributes into a large set of interfaces and classes. Some of the things that you can do using this package that you cannot do using the File class are - open files in ten different read/write modes, copy files and directories, refer to files in a different file systems, watch directories for changes at run time, get finer control over symbolic links, recursively traversing directories, synchronize writes to the file system, and managing file attributes. Yes, that’s a lot of things and there are more! But do not worry. You are not expected to know all of these things. Usually developers learn how to perform these tasks only when they actually need them on a project.

The OCP exam also does not expect you to know about most of the above mentioned features and focuses only on one feature - working with paths and their properties.

19.2.1 Important classes and interfaces of the java.nio.file package☝

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
421

The following is a list of interfaces and classes from the java.nio.file package that you need to be aware of for the OCP exam.

Interface Important Methods

Path

Instance methods:

getName(int index), getParent(), getRoot(), subpath(int beginIndex, int endIndex), toAbsolutePath(),

toRealPath(LinkOption... options), toFile()

normalize(), relativize(Path other), resolve(Path other)

Static method:

of(String first, String... more)

OpenOption

CopyOption
Class Important Methods

FileSystems

Contains only static factory methods for file systems.

Static methods:

getDefault(), getFileSystem(URI uri)

FileSystem

Provides an interface to a file system and is the factory for objects to access files and other objects in the file system.

Instance methods:

getPath(String first, String... more),

getRootDirectories(), getSeparator()

Paths (Not recommended for use anymore)

Contains only static methods that return a Path by converting a path string or a URI.

Static methods:

get(String first, String... more), get(URI uri)
ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
422

Files

Contains only static methods that operate on files and directories.

Methods to copy, create, delete, and move files as well as directories.

Methods to list a directory - Stream<Path> list(Path dir)

Methods to read character data such as Stream<String> lines(Path path), List<String> readAllLines(Path path), String readString(Path p)

Methods to walk a directory tree - Stream<Path> walk(Path start, int maxDepth, FileVisitOption... options) and Stream<Path> walk(Path start, FileVisitOption... options)

Methods to read binary data such as byte[] readAllBytes(Path p)

Methods to get stream based readers and writers for binary and character data such as:

InputStream newInputStream(Path path, OpenOption... options), OutputStream newOutputStream(Path path, OpenOption... options), newBufferedReader(Path path), and newBufferedWriter(Path path, OpenOption... options)

Methods to write character and binary data to a file such as write(Path path, Iterable lines, OpenOption... options), writeString(Path path, CharSequence csq, OpenOption... options), write(Path path, byte[] bytes, OpenOption... options)

Several isXXX() methods to enquire file attributes such as isRegularFile, isReadable, isWritable, isHidden, and isSymbolicLink.
ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
423

enum StandardOpenOption

implements OpenOption

Defines 10 modes: READ, WRITE, APPEND, TRUNCATE_EXISTING, CREATE, CREATE_NEW, DELETE_ON_CLOSE, DSYNC, SYNC, SPARSE

enum StandardCopyOption

implements CopyOption

Defines 3 modes: ATOMIC_MOVE, COPY_ATTRIBUTES, REPLACE_EXISTING

enum LinkOption implements

OpenOption, CopyOption

Defines 1 mode: NOFOLLOW_LINKS

19.2.2 Using the Path interface☝

You have already seen file names and paths in the previous section. They were all just strings and they used platform specific separators. The java.nio.file package formally abstracts the concept of a path using the java.nio.file.Path interface. Other classes of this package use Path objects instead of strings whenever they need to refer to a file.

There is no publicly accessible class that implements the Path interface, which means, you cannot instantiate Path objects directly. You need to get Path objects using either an existing Path object or by using the static of(...) methods of the Path interface.

The following example shows the usage of a few commonly used methods of the Path interface. The output is (shown in comments) assumes that it is run from C:\temp :

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
424

The following are a few points worth noting about the above example:

  1. The of method provides a way to convert a String into a Path object. The strings used to create Path objects follow the same conventions and syntax as the ones you saw in the previous section. Thus, ./files is a relative path because it doesn't start with a root component. It will be interpreted as being relative to the current directory, which is C:\temp in this case.

  2. The getFileName method returns the farthest (i.e. the last) element of the path, which is files in this case.

  3. The subpath method returns a relative path that is a subsequence of the name elements of this path. The returned Path object has the name elements that begin at beginIndex and extend to the element at index endIndex-1. Note that it returns a relative path, which means, the returned path will never start with the root.

  4. The getRoot method returns the root if it is invoked on an absolute path. Since ./files is not an absolute path, getRoot returns null here.

  5. The toAbsolutePath() method returns the full path of the given relative path starting from the default root. In this case, the root is C:\ and so, it returns C:\temp\.\files . Observe that this path contains a . , which is redundant. This shows that the toAbsolutePath method does not return the canonical version of the path. To get that, we need to call normalize() . The normalize() method is practically the same as the getCanonicalPath method of the java.io.File class (except the fact that normalize() returns a Path object instead of a String ).

  6. The toRealPath method returns the canonical path for the given path and also checks if the file (or the directory) referred to by the path actually exists. If the file does not exist, it throws an IOException . Thus, in this example, if the files directory exists, it will return C:\temp\files and if it does not, it will throw an IOException . (In reality, a java.nio.file.NoSuchFileException is thrown but since the JavaDoc says it throws IOException , we will go with IOException . NoSuchFileException is an IOException .) In fact, this is one of the only

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
425

  1. two methods of the Path interface that can throw an IOException . The other method is register , but it is not in scope for this exam.

Not important for the exam, but it is good to know that a well formed paths do not end with a trailing separator character (unless it is referring to the root such as C:\ or / ). So, even if you try to create a Path with a trailing slash, it will be ignored. For example:

System.out.print(Path.of("/a/b/"));  will print /a/b  and not /a/b/ .

Using resolve and resolveSibling☝

Absolute paths on different environments in which an application runs, seldom match. For example, let’s say your application needs to read a properties file named values.properties .

On your Windows machine, you may be running this application under C:\dev\projects\fintech  and this file may be located at C:\dev\projects\fintech\props\values.properties . But in  production, the application may be running under /home/ec2-user/prod/fintech  and the actual location of this file may be /home/ec2-user/prod/fintech/props/values.properties . Obviously, if you hardcode the absolute path to this file in your code based on your development environment, the application will not be able to find this file in production. Therefore, it is advisable to use relative paths instead of absolute paths in Java code. The base path is provided to the application though a command line argument and the application “resolves” the relative paths (which are same on all environments) relative to the base path whenever it needs to locate the file in the file system. The following example shows how it is done:

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
426

You can create and run the above TestClass.java  file from any directory but run it with the following command:

java -Dbasepath=c:\temp TestClass

The -D  option passes a system property named basepath  with a value of c:\temp  to the program, which the program can read by calling System.getProperty("basepath") .

If the argument to the resolve  method is a relative path, the resolve  method assumes that the given path is relative to the path on which this method is called. Therefore, it just joins the two paths to create the actual path to the file. In this case, since basePath  is c:\temp , basePath.resolve("props\values.properties");  returns c:\temp\props\values.properties . If the argument is an absolute path, there is nothing to resolve and it returns the same path as the argument.

The resolveSibling  method works similarly. The only difference is, it assumes that the path given as an argument is supposed to exist at the same level as the path on which this method is invoked. Therefore, propFilePath.resolveSibling("dbconnection.properties")  returns c:\temp\props\dbconnection.properties . Had we called propFilePath.resolve("dbconnection.properties") , it would have returned c:\temp\props\values.properties\dbconnection.properties .

Finding relative path using relativize☝

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
427

The relativize  method is the inverse of resolve . It finds a path to the given file relative to the path on which it is invoked. In other words, it tells you the path that you need to take to reach the given path from the path on which it is called. Continuing the previous example, given that basePath  is c:\temp  and that propFilePath  is c:\temp\props\values.properties , basePath.relativize(propFilePath)  will return props/values.properties .

Although the exam does not have tricky questions on this topic, the following paragraph from the JavaDoc description of this method is worth reading:

“A relative path cannot be constructed if only one of the paths have a root component. Where both paths have a root component then it is implementation dependent if a relative path can be constructed. If this path and the given path are equal then an empty path is returned. For any two normalized paths p  and q , where q  does not have a root component,  p.relativize(p.resolve(q)).equals(q) .”

Ideally, the resolve  and relativize  methods make sense when they are invoked on paths representing a directory (the argument can be a file or a directory). Since a Path  object makes no distinction between a file and a directory, you can invoke these methods even if the Path  you invoke them on represents a file. However, computing the result of these methods mentally becomes a bit confusing when the Path  represents a file. For example:

In the above code, you are trying to go from file1Path  to

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
428

file2Path  and relativize  returns ../log.txt . If you actually try to use the result as a series of steps that you need to take to reach log.txt  from values.properties  on the command line, you will find that the steps are incorrect. The ..  expects to you step up one directory and then find log.txt . However, values.properties  is actually a file and not a directory. You cannot be inside values.properties  to begin with. You can only be in the props  directory and if you go one level up from the props  directory, you will be in the temp  directory, which does not have log.txt .

Therefore, while working out the result you need to assume that the path is a directory and the steps are given assuming you are in that directory. Thus, if you are, hypothetically, in the values.properties  directory, you need to go up one directory to props , and then you will find log.txt  there.

File and Path interoperability☝

If you want work exclusively with the Path  interface, you can. You will not need to use the old java.io.File  class for anything. However, old code and third party libraries that your code depends on may still be using the File  class. Both - the File  class and the Path  interface - have methods that allow you to switch between the two easily. Specifically, File  has a toPath  method that returns a Path  object representing the same file and Path  has a toFile  method that returns the corresponding File  object.

19.2.3 Using the Files class☝

While going through the methods in the java.nio.file.Files  class, you will notice that it is a convenience class that offers a ton of methods and they let you do pretty much everything that you may want to do with files. In fact, all of its methods are static and they delegate to the associated file system provider to perform file

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
429

operations.

Although the exam does not focus directly on this class, a few of its methods appear frequently in the questions. I will go over only such methods in this section. Just remember that this class uses Path instead of a String to refer to a file.

Getting I/O streams☝

The Files class has several overloaded methods that allow you to acquire binary as well as character streams from a file. They are easy to remember because the names and parameters of these methods follow a pattern. Their names start with new and their first parameter is always the Path to the file that you want to access. Depending on which overloaded method you are using, they have one or two more parameters - a Charset, in case you are trying to acquire a character stream, and a varargs array of OpenOptions that determine the mode in which the file is to be opened. The following are a few examples:

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
430

Most of the parameters are self explanatory but the OpenOption parameter is a bit complicated. OpenOption is an interface and it does not define any value. The values are defined in the StandardOpenOption class, which implements OpenOption. All four methods mentioned above can be invoked without this argument and in that case, a default value is assumed. The defaults are different for each method and are as follows:

  • For newInputStream, the default is READ.
  • For newOutputStream, the default is the combination of WRITE, CREATE, and TRUNCATE_EXISTING.
  • The newBufferedReader method does not have this parameter because the file is opened only for reading.
  • For newBufferedWriter, the default is the combination of WRITE, CREATE, and TRUNCATE_EXISTING.

The combination of WRITE, CREATE, and TRUNCATE_EXISTING means that the file is opened for writing, it will be created if it does not already exist, and it will be truncated (i.e. all of its contents will be deleted) if it exists.

Furthermore, depending on whether you are opening a file for reading or writing, some OpenOption combinations may be illegal to use while acquiring the stream. For example, if you pass StandardOpenOption.WRITE to newInputStream, or StandardOpenOption.READ to newOutputStream an IllegalArgumentException will be thrown. You don’t need to remember all combinations of valid and invalid values for the exam.

Reading and writing all data at once☝

If you feel that using streams to read or write small files is too much work, you will love the following methods:

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
431

Reading and writing bytes

Reading and writing text

Not only are the above methods convenient to use, they also close the underlying resource automatically after the reading/writing is complete without the need for wrapping them into a try-with-resource statement. The only catch is that if you try them on a large file, you may run out of memory.

Creating a Stream of lines☝

Since reading all the lines using the readAllLines method runs the risk of running out of memory, a better approach is to create a java.util.stream.Stream of lines out of the text file using the lines method:
