Chapter 24

Implementing Localization

Chapter Overview

This chapter covers the following exam objectives: Implement localization using locales, resource bundles, parse and format messages, dates, times, and numbers including currency and percentage values

Exam Objectives Covered

  • Implement localization using locales, resource bundles, parse and format messages, dates, times, and numbers including currency and percentage values
ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION

Chapter 24 Implementing Localization

729

729

Exam Objectives

  • Implement localization using locales, resource bundles, parse and format messages, dates, times, and numbers including currency and percentage values

24.1 Understanding localization☝

While developing an application it is easy to forget that the application may be used by people in different countries with different languages. At a bare minimum, an application is expected to display information meant for the consumption of the user in the language that the user can understand. An application usually produces user consumable information in the form of UI control labels such as OK and Cancel buttons, pop up messages, and, any pro-forma text that goes in the output produced upon the user's request such as PDF/Word reports. Since the regions and languages of the users of the application cannot always be known at the time of development of the application, it is almost impossible to hardcode region specific formats and texts in the application itself. Thus, instead of trying to do that, it is better for the application to not assume any language at all and use the facilities of the run time environment to localize the messages. The runtime environment can easily format information such as dates and numbers as per the local format but it cannot translate arbitrary application specific messages to the local language. To manage that, the application must use message "keys" that can be looked up into a region and language specific "resource bundle" at runtime and use the returned values to generate the messages.

An application is packaged with multiple resource bundles, but at run time, it detects

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
730

the user's region and uses the resource bundle specific to that region to translate the messages.

This approach allows the application code to be written in a completely language neutral fashion and also allows the creation of the region specific resource bundles to be handled by a team that knows the local language. Such an application does not require any code change to be ported into different regions and is, therefore, called "internationalized". Refactoring an application to remove any region specific hardcoding from the application code is called "internationalization" or "i18n" in short (because there are 18 characters between i and n in the word internationalization) and the process of creating and packaging region specific files, i.e., resource bundles, for a particular region or language for the application is called "localization".

24.1.1 Detecting a user's Locale☝

A user's region (the word region and country are used interchangeably in this context) and language specific information is captured by the java.util.Locale class. It has several methods such as getCountry() and getLanguage() that return specific details of the region that it represents. You are not expected to know the actual values that these methods return for any locale, but be aware that most of these methods return well defined codes. For example, they return "en" and "US" for language and country if the Locale represents a region in the US that speaks English. Note that the language is a string of 2 to 8 letters in lowercase and the country is a string of 2 or 3 letters in uppercase.

A Locale also captures a "category" that tells you whether this locale information is meant for customizing UI displays or for customizing textual output generated by the application. This category is represented by a static nested enum defined in Locale named Category. It has just two constants - DISPLAY and FORMAT. Category is helpful when the application needs to make a distinction between the two types of customizations. For example, an application may want to display UI controls in local language Hindi but wants to generate reports in English. For the purpose of the OCP exam, however, this distinction is not important but it should be kept in mind while building a professional application. Also remember that whenever the category is not

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
731

specified, FORMAT is assumed.

User's locale can be retrieved using the static methods getDefault() or getDefault(Locale.Category) of Locale. Many applications provide the user with an option to change their default locale. This can be done using Locale.setDefault(Locale newLocale) and Locale.setDefault(Locale.Category category, Locale newLocale). These methods change the default locale of the JVM and any subsequent calls to getDefault will return the new locale.

Creating a Locale☝

A Locale object can be created using Locale.Builder or using overloaded static factory methods named of of the Locale class. Locale's constructors are deprecated and can be ignored. Here are a few examples that you might see in the exam:

Observe that Builder's has a setRegion (and not setCountry) method but this API treats region and country as the same. Locale also defines several public static constants for countries and languages for example, Locale.US and Locale.CHINESE.

An important point to note about Locale is that it may not always have values for all of the details of the location that it represents. For example, Locale.CHINESE is a Locale that represents just the CHINESE language. Calling Locale.CHINESE.getCountry() will return an empty string (not null). Similarly, a calling getLanguage() on a Locale built using new Locale.Builder().setRegion("US").build() will return "".

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
732

Locale overrides the toString method to return a string representation of the Locale. As per JavaDoc, it returns language + "_" + country + "_" + (variant + "_#" | "#") + script + "_" + extensions. Language is always lower case, country is always upper case, script is always title case, and extensions are always lower case. In the exam, you will see Locales that use only the language and the country and their string representations will be like en_US or fr_CA.

24.2 Localizing Numbers and Dates☝

Using NumberFormat

People in different regions are accustomed to seeing numbers formatted differently. The JDK provides an abstract class java.text.NumberFormatter that can format a number as per the local standard. The following example shows how it can be used:

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
733

The NumberFormat class has several static factory methods that can be used to create a NumberFormat instance. Most of them have overloaded variants that take a Locale as an argument. The ones that do not take a Locale, return an instance that formats a number according to the default locale. Note that the instances returned by these methods are not thread safe.

The exam does not expect you to know the exact format in which numbers are printed for different locales but it does expect you to know the currency symbols for US Dollar($), Euro(€), and GB Pound(£).

Parsing Numbers

A NumberFormat instance can also be used to parse a String containing a number written in locale specific manner. For example, nfC.parse("1M"), where nfC points to the same NumberFormat instance used in the above example, will return a Long containing the value 1000000. Be aware that the return type of the parse method is Number but it may actually return a Double or a Long depending on whether the string has digits after decimal or not. Also remember that this method can potentially throw a java.text.ParseException, which is a checked exception.

Using DateTimeFormatter

To format the LocalDate/Time objects of the java.time package, you need to use the java.time.format.DateTimeFormatter class. It has three different methods,

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
734

ofLocalizedDate, ofLocalizedTime, and ofLocalizedDateTime, which can be used for formatting LocalDate, LocalTime, and LocalDateTime respectively. They require a FormatStyle argument. DateTimeFormatter also has a ofPattern method, which can format the LocalDate/Time objects for any given Locale. This method requires a FormatStyle and a Locale as arguments. For example:

You are not expected to know the formats of various FormatStyle options for the exam. JDK has a java.text.DateFormat class, which is similar to the java.text.NumberFormat and can be used for format dates according the given Locale. However, since this class was created long before the new Date/Time API, it can only format java.util.Date objects. Unfortunately, it hasn't been enhanced to format java.time.LocalDate/Time objects.

Parsing Dates

DateTimeFormatter has several methods that can parse a string into a LocalDate/Time objects. Technically, parsing dates is mentioned in the objectives but parsing dates is quite complicated and we haven't seen anyone getting a question on this. If you have time, you may go through the JavaDoc of the DateTimeFormatter class to learn more about this topic.

24.3 Localizing text☝

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
735

24.3.1 Internationalizing an app using ResourceBundle☝

While developing an internationalized application, all user facing text or words used by the application are identified and listed in file along with their translations in the language that the development team knows in the form of name-value pairs. For example, let's say an application asks the user for their name and then prints that name with a greeting. Something like this:

The application contains two user facing strings - "What is your name?" and "Hi". To internationalize this application, these two strings must not be hardcoded. Instead, the code must use keys to look up the the actual phrases at run time. This is done as follows:

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
736

There are several moving parts in the above code. I will explain all of them but let's get it to run first. If you compile and run it, you will get an exception with the following message:

You may see a different locale name in the output because it depends on the locale of your machine. Now, create a file named helloapp.properties in the same folder that contains HelloApp.class with the following contents:

If you run it now, it will work as expected. The output isn't any different from the previous version but we have accomplished an important thing in the above code. We have managed to look up the keys used in the program and show the resulting values to the user. Although, it still prints messages in English, the application has now been internationalized! All we need to do now is to localize it for the locales in which we want to run this application. But before we do that, let me explain a few points about the above code:

1. A ResourceBundle object contains locale-specific objects. Every resource bundle has a name and the program uses that name to load the relevant files that contain locale specific entries. Once the entries in the files are loaded in the resource bundle, the program can just lookup up a key in that resource bundle and use the returned locale specific value. A resource bundle is not limited to containing just strings but for the purpose of the exam, we will stick to strings only.

2. java.util.ResourceBundle is an abstract class but the JDK also provides a couple of concrete subclasses. One of those subclasses is PropertyResourceBundle, which can load key-value pairs from a properties file. However, instead of directly instantiating the PropertyResourceBundle class, we use ResourceBundle's getBundle method because this method is smart enough to figure out exactly which properties files are needed to create the desired resource bundle based on the user's locale, which is what we want.

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
737

2. The logic to determine the properties files needed to create a resource bundle is important and I will explain it shortly, but in this example, since we don't have any locale specific properties file, the getBundle method just loads a properties file with the same name as that of the resource bundle (but with extension .properties), i.e., helloapp.properties.

3. The name of the resource bundle and the corresponding properties file follows the same convention as the one followed by classes. Therefore, while locating a bundle named helloapp, the getBundle method expects helloapp.properties to be located directly on the classpath. If the name of the bundle were test.helloapp, then helloapp.properties would have to be present in the test folder and the test folder would have to be on the classpath.

4. The name of the keys in the properties file can be anything but the code must use the same key to loop up the value. Therefore, it is a common practice to use strings containing text that makes sense in the language that the dev team understands as keys. Using keys such as key1, key2, makes it hard to tie a key to the text that is meant to be shown to the user for that key.

5. The backslashes (\) in the properties files are required to escape the space character.

24.3.2 Understanding Resource Bundles☝

Resource bundles are hierarchical, meaning, a resource bundle always has a parent bundle unless the bundle is at the top of the hierarchy, in which case, it is called the "base bundle". The hierarchy of resource bundles is established using a set of resource bundles whose names start with a common base name, such as helloapp used in the previous example. The remaining part of the name of a resource bundle is created using various attributes of a locale such as the language and the country separated by underscores. These attributes must always be in the following order:

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
738

For the purpose of the exam, only the language and the country are important but script and variant behave similarly. So, for example, there can be a hierarchy of resource bundles with names helloapp, helloapp_fr, and helloapp_fr_CA, where the bundle named helloapp is at the top (grandparent) and the bundle named helloapp_fr_CA is at the bottom. Note that a bundle named helloapp_fr_FR will also be a part of this hierarchy but it will be on a new branch splitting from helloapp_fr. Similarly, the bundle named helloapp_en_UK will also be a separate branch splitting off of helloapp_en but if helloapp_en does not exist then helloapp_en_UK will be the immediate descendant of helloapp. This is illustrated in the following image:

All of these resource bundles belong to the same family of resource bundles because they share the same base name, i.e., helloapp.

Understanding the hierarchy of resource bundles is important because when a key is not found in a particular bundle, it is automatically searched for in the parent bundle up to the base bundle. When a key is not found in any of the ancestor bundles either, a MissingResourceException is thrown. So, if helloapp_fr is queried for a key that it does not have, its parent bundle hellopapp will be checked and if helloapp too doesn't have that key then a MissingResourceException will be thrown. Note that the search for a key only propagates up the hierarchy and never to a child or a sibling.

So now, based on the above discussion, can you tell what resource bundle we should

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
739

have if, for example, we want to localize our app for French speaking people in Canada?

Right, hellopapp_fr_CA indeed. But remember that the values from this bundle will not be used for French speaking people in France because their locale would be fr_FR and they would search for the keys in helloapp_fr_FR. If you check the above graph, helloapp_fr_CA is a sibling and not a parent of helloapp_fr_FR. However, if we have resource bundle named helloapp_fr instead of helloapp_fr_CA, then the values from this bundle can be used for all French speaking locales because helloapp_fr is a parent of helloapp_fr_CA as well as helloapp_fr_FR.

24.3.3 Creating resource bundles using properties files☝

The ResourceBundle class has several overloaded static getBundle methods out of which two are particularly important - getBundle(String baseName) and getBundle(String baseName, Locale locale). The former returns a ResourceBundle for the user's default locale while the later returns a ResourceBundle for the given locale. The baseName is the common name of the all the resource bundles of a family as explained before.

Requesting a resource bundle for the default locale☝

The getBundle methods automatically read a properties file whose name matches with the name of the requested resource bundle (provided the properties file is on the classpath) and create a PropertyResourceBundle instance that contains the entries defined in the properties file. So, for example, assuming that the user's default locale is en_US, the call to ResourceBundle.getBundle("helloapp"), will look for a properties file named helloapp_en_US.properties file in the classpath and will load the entries into the helloapp_en_US bundle. Remember that since resource bundles are hierarchical, creation of a ResourceBundle triggers the creation of its parent bundle as well. Therefore, once helloapp_en_US bundle is created, its parent bundle helloapp_en would also be created automatically in a

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
740

similar fashion. Thus, it will look for helloapp_en.properties to create the helloapp_en bundle. Similarly, since the parent of helloapp_en is helloapp, helloapp.properties will also be loaded.

If, while creating the hierarchy of the resource bundles, at any stage, the getBundle method is not able to find the matching properties file, it tries to create the parent bundle of the requested bundle and returns that parent bundle instead. So, if helloapp_hi_IN.properties is not available, getBundle("helloapp", Locale.of("hi", "IN")) method will try to create the parent bundle, i.e., helloapp_hi as per the process described above and return this bundle instead.

Effectively, the call to getBundle causes the creation of the hierarchy from the requested bundle up to the base bundle (but not the children or siblings) using properties files. The following image illustrates the search path that getBundle takes in this example:

Requesting a resource bundle for the non-default locale☝

The process for creating a requested bundle for a non-default locale is almost the same as above. The only difference is that when getBundle is not able to find any locale specific properties file, it doesn't immediately return the base resource bundle. Before returning the base bundle, it tries to find and return the bundle for the default

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
741

locale. So, for example, let's say the default locale of the user is en_US and you request a bundle for hi_IN locale by calling getBundle("helloapp", Locale.of("hi", "IN")). Now, if this method is not able to find any properties file that matches hi_IN locale (i.e. helloapp_hi_IN.properties or helloapp_hi.properties), it will try to create and return the bundle for the default locale, i.e., helloapp_en_US. If it is not able to find any properties file for en_US locale either, it will return the base bundle, i.e., helloapp. The following image illustrates the search path that getBundle takes in this example:

At this point, you might be curious to know what happens if getBundle is not able to find any properties file that it can use to create any bundle at all. Well, in that case it throws a MissingResourceException.

The exam does not try to trick you on the location of the properties files. Unless the problem statement states otherwise, you can safely assume that the properties file given in the question is on the classpath and can be loaded by a ResourceBundle. You just need to check the name of the properties file and the name of the resource bundle being requested using the getBundle method.

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
742

24.3.4 Looking up a key in a ResourceBundle☝

If you have read the previous two sections carefully, this section will be like a walk in the park.

To look up a key in a ResourceBundle, you need to first get hold of a ResourceBundle that you are interested in using one of the getBundle methods and then call one of the following three getXXX methods on that resource bundle:

String getString(String key): Gets a string for the given key from this resource bundle or one of its parents.

String[] getStringArray(String key): Gets a string array for the given key from this resource bundle or one of its parents.

Object getObject(String key): Gets an object for the given key from this resource bundle or one of its parents.

Only the first one is important for the exam and you have already seen the example that uses this method earlier. As you must have observed in the short descriptions of the above methods, they return a value from this bundle or from one of its parents.

The exam tests you on whether you are able to figure out the value for a key for a non default locale when the key is not present in the properties file for the non-default locale but is present in the properties file of the default locale. You need to remember that loading a resource bundle and looking up the key in a resource bundle are two separate and independent steps. If a properties file for the non-default locale is present, then a resource bundle will be created using that file as explained in the previous section. The key lookup in the non-default bundle will happen in this non-default bundle hierarchy only and if the key is not found in it, a MissingResourceException will be thrown. The key will NOT be looked up in the resource bundle hierarchy of the default locale. The resource bundle hierarchy of the default locale is used only when no locale specific resource bundle is found.

Ideally, the application code shouldn't need to pass in a Locale to get the bundle. It should just pass the base name of the

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
743

bundle and get the bundle for the default locale of the application because passing a locale defeats the purpose of i18n.

The application should split the entries into multiple bundles though for easier management of the entries. For example, you could create separate resource bundles for each of the functionally different parts of an application. Note that an application may have any number of resource bundles.

24.3.5 Formatting Messages using MessageFormat☝

Showing messages that are grammatically correct in different locales is tricky. For example, a sentence such as "Your balance is 100 points." would be grammatically correct in many languages such as English. However, it would be grammatically incorrect in many other languages such as Hindi, in which the correct form would be, "Your balance 100 points is".

Thus, if the program creates this message using string concatenation such as String msg = rb.getString("yourbalanceis")+" "+amount+" "+rb.getString("points");, the resulting string may look odd in some locales.

The java.text.MessageFormat class can be used to solve this problem. Instead of hardcoding string concatenation, you can specify parameters in the message string and, at run time, format the string by passing values for those parameters. For example:

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
744

The above code uses the static String format(String pattern, Object... arguments) method of MessageFormat. The value for yourbalanceis key will be Your balance is {0}. in helloapp_en_us.properties but Your balance {0} is. (but in Hindi, of course) in helloapp_hi_IN.properties.

Using the MessageFormat class is quite simple. The parameters are specified using curly braces and besides the parameter index (indexing starts with zero), you may specify how you want to format it as well. For example, a raw message could be, "On {0, date, short}, your balance was {1, number, currency}" and it can be formatted using an array of two objects as arguments like this: MessageFormat.format(msg, new Object[]{new Date(), 100});

This will return "On 10/05/24, your balance was $100.00.".

A serious issue with MessageFormat is that it was created before the introduction of the java.time package and hasn't been been updated to work with the new LocalDate/Time classes. So, if you want to use dates as arguments, you have to use java.util.Date objects.

24.3.6 Quiz☝

Q. Assume that the default locale of the application is en_US and the following two properties

files are present on the classpath:

and

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
745

What will be the output of the following code snippet?

Answer: Hi Hola Hi Hi Regards Regards <MissingResourceException> (ignoring new lines)

Observe the following points:

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
746

1. Since the default locale is en_US, whenever you request a bundle without specifying the locale (as in //1) or for a locale for which no locale specific properties file is present (as in //3, //4, //5, and //6), the bundle for the default locale, which is created using msgs_en_US.properties will be returned. Thus, //1, //3, and //4 will print Hi, and //5 and //6 will print Regards.

2. Asking for a bundle for es_MX as in //2 and //7 will return the es_MX bundle because msgs_es_MX.properties is present. The default bundle will not be used at all if you look for keys in this bundle. Therefore, //2 will print Hola but //7 will throw a MissingResourceException because the es_MX bundle doesn't contain the key regards.

In the exam, you may encounter a badly written question that does not explicitly specify the default locale of the application. In such a case, you may assume the default locale to be en_US but only if it is absolutely necessary to arrive at the correct answer.

24.4 Exercise☝

1. Write a program that detects and prints the details such as the language and country of its default locale. Change the default locale to something else.

2. Input a number with decimals and print its localized string version for the default locale as if it were a monetary amount and also as if it were a percentage value.

3. Create a family of resource bundles for two different locales (one for the default and one for any another locale) with base name com.abc.reports using properties files. Add a key named goodbye to all of them. Retrieve the locale specific value for this key from the program for the default as well as the other

ORACLE CERTIFIED PROFESSIONAL • JAVA SE 17 & 21 CERTIFICATION
747

3. locale.
